subtopic_detail: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose with QRS widening
stem: You are the emergency registrar called to resus for a 32-year-old non-Aboriginal woman brought in 90 minutes after a deliberate overdose of approximately 50 amitriptyline 50 mg tablets. She is drowsy with a GCS of 11. Her partner found her with the empty bottle. She has a 5-year history of depression and previous self-harm by cutting. There is no other prescribed medication and no recreational drug use disclosed.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.5 °C, HR 132 sinus tachycardia, RR 16, SpO2 96% room air, BP 92/56 mmHg, GCS 11 (eyes 2, verbal 4, motor 5)
data_table.Examination: Dry mouth, mydriasis with sluggish pupils, dry warm skin, urinary retention, hyperreflexia, no clonus
data_table.Investigations: ECG QRS 132 ms with terminal R wave in aVR over 3 mm, QTc 480 ms, ABG pH 7.30 pCO2 30 mmHg HCO3 17 mmol/L, paracetamol level under 10 mg/L, alcohol negative, salicylate not detected, potassium 3.4 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus targeting QRS narrowing and arterial pH 7.45 to 7.55, intubate to protect the airway, and arrange ICU admission
options.rationale: QRS over 100 ms with terminal R in aVR over 3 mm signals significant TCA cardiotoxicity; sodium bicarbonate narrows QRS by sodium loading and pH-driven protein binding shift, intubation protects the airway with deteriorating GCS, and ICU is mandatory. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Toxinology)
source_refs: Australasian College for Emergency Medicine Toxicology Reference

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 50 g orally, place on telemetry in the resus bay, and reassess the ECG and conscious state in 2 hours before deciding on further intervention
options.rationale: Charcoal at 90 minutes after overdose has limited benefit and is contraindicated with a falling GCS without airway protection; QRS widening already mandates bicarbonate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Toxinology)

options.text: Administer intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg over 30 seconds for the reduced level of consciousness and reassess airway protective reflexes after each dose titration
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed overdose with TCA because it can precipitate seizures by lowering the seizure threshold against the proconvulsant TCA effect. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Toxinology)

options.text: Administer intravenous physostigmine 1 mg slow push to reverse the anticholinergic syndrome and arrange continuous cardiac monitoring with intensive care liaison after dose
options.rationale: Physostigmine is contraindicated in TCA overdose due to the risk of asystole; reversal of anticholinergic features does not address the sodium channel blockade. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Toxinology)

options.text: Administer intravenous amiodarone 300 mg over 20 minutes for the sinus tachycardia and broadening QRS, continue oxygen and intravenous fluids, and admit to a monitored ward bed
options.rationale: Class IA, IC and III antiarrhythmics worsen sodium channel blockade and are contraindicated in TCA toxicity; treatment is sodium bicarbonate and supportive care. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology and Toxinology)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes sodium channel blockade (wide QRS, terminal R in aVR), QT prolongation, hypotension, anticholinergic delirium, seizures and arrhythmias. Sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg is the antidote, narrowing QRS by sodium loading and alkalosis-driven protein binding. Airway protection precedes any procedure on a falling GCS. Class IA, IC and III antiarrhythmics, physostigmine and flumazenil are contraindicated.
key_points: QRS over 100 ms or terminal R in aVR over 3 mm signals significant TCA cardiotoxicity
Sodium bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg is the antidote targeting QRS narrowing and pH 7.45 to 7.55
Intubate before lavage or charcoal if GCS is falling
Avoid class IA, IC and III antiarrhythmics, physostigmine and flumazenil
ICU admission until ECG is normal off bicarbonate for at least 6 hours
explanation.pearls: Intravenous lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg of 20% solution is a rescue therapy for refractory cardiovascular collapse from lipophilic drug toxicity including amitriptyline, and may be considered when bicarbonate and standard resuscitation are insufficient.

sources.label: Australasian College for Emergency Medicine Toxicology Reference

stem: You are the on-call psychiatry registrar in ED with a 26-year-old non-Aboriginal man brought in by police for violent and threatening behaviour in the community. He has a 3-year history of schizophrenia. He is shouting, intermittently striking the walls and refuses oral medication. De-escalation by experienced ED nursing staff has not reduced his agitation. He has no known medical conditions, no allergies and is otherwise physically well. Six security staff are present.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 124, RR 22, SpO2 99% room air, BP 142/88 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Highly agitated, no signs of intoxication, no head injury, no needle marks, no pyrexia, no clonus, no rigidity
data_table.Investigations: Bedside glucose 5.4 mmol/L, urine drug screen pending, ECG QTc 410 ms on previous admission

lead_in: What is the single best initial pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Intramuscular haloperidol 5 mg with promethazine 25 mg as a combined dose, no cardiac monitoring required, and admit to the psychiatric assessment unit immediately afterwards
options.rationale: Promethazine combinations are no longer preferred in Australian guidance due to lower efficacy than droperidol; cardiac monitoring is required for all parenteral antipsychotic use in this setting. (Source: NSW Health Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance)
source_refs: NSW Health Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance

options.text: Intramuscular midazolam 10 mg as a single dose without antipsychotic, observe airway and oximetry in the resus bay, and repeat at 10 minute intervals as needed
options.rationale: Benzodiazepine monotherapy at 10 mg carries higher airway and oversedation risks than droperidol at this dose in adult acute behavioural disturbance; combination or antipsychotic monotherapy is preferred. (Source: NSW Health Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance)
source_refs: NSW Health Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance

options.text: Oral olanzapine 20 mg wafer with lorazepam 2 mg by mouth and observe in the cubicle, repeating doses at 30 minute intervals if cooperation continues to improve
options.rationale: He has refused oral medication; oral routes are not appropriate in active behavioural disturbance requiring tranquillisation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Intramuscular droperidol 10 mg as a single dose with continuous pulse oximetry and cardiac monitoring, repeat dose available at 15 minutes if needed
options.rationale: Australian rapid tranquillisation guidance recommends intramuscular droperidol 10 mg as first-line for adult acute behavioural disturbance with cardiac monitoring; large RCT data (DORM trial) support efficacy and safety. (Source: NSW Health Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance)
source_refs: NSW Health Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance

options.text: Intravenous ketamine 100 mg as a sedative dose with anaesthetic standby, intubation kit prepared, and direct admission to ICU once sedated
options.rationale: Ketamine is a salvage option for failure of standard rapid tranquillisation, not first-line; its use mandates anaesthetic backup and is not the initial step. (Source: NSW Health Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance)
source_refs: NSW Health Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance

explanation.summary: Acute severe behavioural disturbance in Australia uses parenteral rapid tranquillisation when de-escalation fails. Intramuscular droperidol 10 mg is first-line in adults with cardiac monitoring and oximetry; the Druid 1 and Druid 2 trial programme supports efficacy and acceptable QTc safety. Olanzapine intramuscular 10 mg is an alternative. Ketamine is reserved for refractory cases. Mechanical restraint and post-tranquillisation observation are mandatory components.
key_points: IM droperidol 10 mg is the Australian first-line for adult acute behavioural disturbance
Cardiac monitoring and oximetry are mandatory during and after rapid tranquillisation
Olanzapine IM 10 mg is an alternative first-line in adults
Ketamine is reserved for refractory cases with anaesthetic backup
De-escalation precedes pharmacological intervention; restraint is the last resort
explanation.pearls: Combined intramuscular olanzapine and benzodiazepines within 60 minutes of each other are associated with hypotension and respiratory depression; if recent IM lorazepam or midazolam has been given, droperidol is preferred over olanzapine.

sources.label: NSW Health Management of Patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance

subtopic_detail: Adult ADHD with comorbid stimulant use disorder
stem: You are the addiction psychiatry registrar in a tertiary clinic seeing a 29-year-old non-Aboriginal man referred for assessment of adult ADHD with comorbid methamphetamine use disorder. He has used methamphetamine intermittently for 6 years and was abstinent for the past 4 weeks following a residential withdrawal admission. He describes lifelong inattention, distractibility and impulsivity, with school records confirming attentional difficulties from age 8. His mother has untreated ADHD. He is engaged with an outpatient drug and alcohol service. He drives a delivery truck for work.

data_table.Examination: Normal cardiovascular and neurological examination, no signs of intoxication or withdrawal, no peripheral stigmata of long-term stimulant use
data_table.Investigations: Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS) score 18 of 24 inattentive features, structured diagnostic interview meets DSM-5 ADHD criteria with childhood symptom evidence, urine drug screen negative, ECG QTc 405 ms with normal sinus rhythm, FBC, UEC, LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacological plan?

options.text: Commence guanfacine extended release 4 mg daily as a non-stimulant alternative, with the understanding that this is the preferred non-stimulant choice over atomoxetine in adults
options.rationale: Guanfacine ER is approved in Australia for children and adolescents but is not first-line for adult ADHD; atomoxetine is the standard adult non-stimulant. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Commence immediate-release dexamfetamine 5 mg twice daily and titrate weekly to symptom response, with monthly urine drug screens and direct supply through a single prescriber
options.rationale: Immediate-release stimulants carry the highest diversion and misuse risk and are not first-line in active or recent stimulant use disorder. Long-acting formulations are preferred if a stimulant trial is later required. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Commence lisdexamfetamine 30 mg morning dose immediately with daily pharmacy dispensing and weekly urine screens, plus continued outpatient drug and alcohol engagement throughout therapy
options.rationale: Although long-acting stimulants have lower diversion risk than immediate-release, Australian guidance recommends a period of demonstrated stability and non-stimulant trial first in comorbid stimulant use disorder. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Defer ADHD pharmacotherapy until 12 months of abstinence, continue cognitive behavioural therapy and addiction support, and review at the end of that period for treatment planning
options.rationale: Indefinite deferral worsens ADHD symptom burden and risks return to methamphetamine self-medication; non-stimulant trial can proceed safely with addiction medicine engagement. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

options.text: Commence atomoxetine titrated to 80 mg daily, continue addiction medicine engagement, and reassess in 8 weeks before considering stimulant trial if symptoms persist
options.rationale: Australian ADHD guidelines recommend non-stimulant first-line treatment in adults with comorbid stimulant use disorder due to diversion and relapse risk; atomoxetine is the standard non-stimulant choice. Reassessment after substance-use stabilisation guides any later stimulant decision. (Source: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD)
source_refs: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

explanation.summary: ADHD and stimulant use disorder co-occur frequently and each worsens the other if untreated. Australian guidance prioritises non-stimulant pharmacotherapy (atomoxetine, less commonly bupropion) in adults with active or recent stimulant use disorder, with structured addiction medicine engagement throughout. Stimulant trial may be revisited after demonstrated stability, using long-acting formulations and tight prescriber controls. Driving capacity, cardiovascular screening and contraceptive counselling are part of standard ADHD initiation.
key_points: Atomoxetine is first-line for adult ADHD with comorbid stimulant use disorder
Indefinite deferral of pharmacotherapy worsens outcomes
Long-acting stimulants are preferred over immediate-release if later required
Baseline cardiovascular and driving screening is essential
Concurrent addiction medicine engagement is mandatory
explanation.pearls: Atomoxetine titration typically takes 4 to 6 weeks for full clinical effect, unlike stimulants which act within hours; this slow onset is a useful counselling point because patients often expect rapid stimulant-like response and disengage if not prepared.

sources.label: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

stem: You are the emergency medicine registrar reviewing a 62-year-old non-Aboriginal man with type 2 diabetes who presents with 18 hours of worsening lower leg pain disproportionate to the visible cellulitis. He is febrile and tachycardic. His pain has progressed despite paracetamol. He has had no recent injury, IV drug use or travel. His diabetes is poorly controlled with an HbA1c of 9.5%. He takes metformin and gliclazide.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9 °C, HR 124, RR 24, BP 96/54 mmHg, SpO2 96% room air
data_table.Examination: Right lower leg with patchy violaceous discolouration, two small skin blisters, exquisite tenderness extending beyond the area of erythema, crepitus on palpation, mild lower leg oedema
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22 x10^9/L, CRP 280 mg/L, lactate 4.1 mmol/L, sodium 132 mmol/L, glucose 16.8 mmol/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L, Hb 122 g/L, LRINEC score 9, plain X-ray shows soft tissue gas

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange MRI of the lower leg before deciding management, withhold antibiotics until diagnosis is confirmed, and reassess in 4 hours for repeat clinical examination by the team
options.rationale: MRI is supportive but should never delay surgical exploration in suspected necrotising fasciitis; antibiotics are started empirically without waiting for imaging. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Admit to the medical ward, commence intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g every 6 hours, mark the erythema boundary and review in 12 hours for response assessment by the medical team
options.rationale: Ward-based monotherapy for presumed cellulitis misses necrotising fasciitis features; delay to debridement increases mortality from around 25% to over 50% beyond 24 hours. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Urgent surgical consultation for theatre debridement, commence broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics including meropenem and clindamycin, and resuscitate with intravenous crystalloid
options.rationale: Pain out of proportion, bullae, crepitus, soft tissue gas and LRINEC 6 or above strongly indicate necrotising fasciitis requiring emergency surgical debridement; broad-spectrum antibiotics including clindamycin for toxin suppression are commenced concurrently. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)
source_refs: ANZ Surgical Site Infection Prevention Guidelines

options.text: Discharge home on oral cephalexin 500 mg four times daily, advise elevation and arrange community nurse review in 24 hours for clinical reassessment of progression
options.rationale: Severe sepsis with hypotension, lactate 4.1 and LRINEC 9 cannot be managed at home; oral cephalexin is inadequate for either severe cellulitis or necrotising fasciitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin 1 g/kg as primary treatment for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome and continue current ward observation pending surgical review in the morning
options.rationale: IVIG is adjunctive in streptococcal toxic shock syndrome but does not replace surgical debridement or empirical antibiotics; delay to theatre is unsafe. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency. Hard signs include pain out of proportion, bullae, crepitus, soft tissue gas and rapid progression. LRINEC score combines CRP, WCC, haemoglobin, sodium, creatinine and glucose; 6 or above is high-risk and 8 or above strongly suggestive. Management is urgent debridement plus broad-spectrum antibiotics including clindamycin for streptococcal toxin suppression. Imaging must never delay theatre.
key_points: Pain out of proportion, bullae, crepitus and gas are necrotising fasciitis red flags
LRINEC 6 or above is high-risk; 8 or above strongly suggests necrotising fasciitis
Urgent surgical debridement is the definitive treatment
Empirical antibiotics include broad-spectrum cover plus clindamycin for toxin suppression
Imaging supports but does not delay surgical exploration
explanation.pearls: The finger test (insertion of a gloved finger through a small skin incision under local anaesthetic) yields easy dissection through subcutaneous tissue without bleeding when necrotising fasciitis is present; a positive bedside finger test is diagnostic and prompts immediate theatre.

sources.label: ANZ Surgical Site Infection Prevention Guidelines

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 48-year-old non-Aboriginal woman in ED with 12 hours of high fever, severe headache, photophobia and confusion. She has been previously well, with no recent travel or immunosuppression. She has had two episodes of vomiting. There is no rash. She works as a primary school teacher.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 °C, HR 122, RR 22, SpO2 97% room air, BP 110/68 mmHg
data_table.Examination: GCS 13, neck stiffness present, no focal neurology, Kernig and Brudzinski positive, no skin rash, no papilloedema
data_table.Investigations: FBC WCC 22 x10^9/L (neutrophils 19), CRP 240 mg/L, lactate 2.6 mmol/L, sodium 135 mmol/L, glucose 6.8 mmol/L, INR 1.1, platelets 215 x10^9/L, blood cultures taken; CT brain pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Perform a lumbar puncture immediately without CT given the absence of focal neurology and papilloedema, send all CSF tubes for analysis, and commence antibiotics after the procedure
options.rationale: Although CT before LP is reserved for specific risk factors, antibiotics should still precede LP when LP is delayed for any reason; sequence matters because of the urgency of antibiotic timing. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Defer antibiotics until lumbar puncture results are available, administer paracetamol 1 g intravenously and dexamethasone 10 mg, and reassess after CSF microscopy
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics must not be delayed beyond 30 minutes for any reason in suspected bacterial meningitis; mortality rises with each hour of delay. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Administer intravenous vancomycin 1.5 g and meropenem 2 g as first-line empirical therapy, given the severity of presentation and risk of cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus in Australia
options.rationale: Vancomycin is added when penicillin or cephalosporin resistance is suspected (e.g. recent foreign travel, recent antibiotic use); empirical first-line in Australia is ceftriaxone plus benzylpenicillin without vancomycin in the absence of those risks. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Administer intravenous dexamethasone 10 mg with the first dose of ceftriaxone 2 g and benzylpenicillin 2.4 g IV, then proceed to lumbar puncture once CT is clear
options.rationale: Empirical ceftriaxone plus benzylpenicillin covers Streptococcus pneumoniae, meningococcus and Listeria for over-50 or vulnerable; dexamethasone given with or just before the first antibiotic dose reduces neurological sequelae in pneumococcal meningitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)
source_refs: ASID Adult Bacterial Meningitis Position Statement

options.text: Administer intravenous aciclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours empirically with ceftriaxone, withhold dexamethasone given the uncertain pathogen, and arrange a lumbar puncture in 6 hours
options.rationale: Aciclovir is added when encephalitis is also suspected; this woman has classic meningitis features. Dexamethasone is given with the first antibiotic dose for suspected bacterial meningitis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Australian empirical therapy for adult community-acquired bacterial meningitis is ceftriaxone 2 g IV every 12 hours plus benzylpenicillin 2.4 g IV every 4 hours, started within 30 minutes of consideration. Dexamethasone 10 mg IV with or just before the first antibiotic dose reduces death and neurological sequelae in pneumococcal disease. CT before LP is reserved for focal neurology, papilloedema, immunocompromise or recent seizure. Listeria cover with benzylpenicillin is included for over-50, pregnant or immunocompromised patients.
key_points: Empirical ceftriaxone plus benzylpenicillin within 30 minutes of consideration
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV with or just before first antibiotic dose for pneumococcal benefit
CT before LP for focal neurology, papilloedema, immunocompromise or recent seizure
Listeria cover with benzylpenicillin for over 50, pregnant, immunocompromised
Continue dexamethasone for 4 days only if CSF or blood cultures grow Streptococcus pneumoniae
explanation.pearls: Dexamethasone reduces unfavourable outcomes only in pneumococcal meningitis and should be stopped at 48 hours if CSF or blood cultures grow another organism or are negative; continuing in non-pneumococcal cases offers no benefit and risks hyperglycaemia.

sources.label: ASID Adult Bacterial Meningitis Position Statement
